name: rag-module
goal: Understand the :rag retrieval module end to end: how documents are chunked, embedded, indexed, retrieved, filtered, and cited. Keep the scope to retrieval only (the LLM lives in :app). Prefer precise, sourced answers over generalities.
questions: What is the RAG module for, and what does it do from a document to an answer?
How does the module split documents into chunks for retrieval?
What does it remember about each chunk so an answer can point back to where it came from?
How does the module turn text and questions into something it can search over?
How does it find the chunks most relevant to a question?
How does the improved pipeline filter out weak matches to give better answers?
Why can rewriting the user's question help retrieval find better chunks?
How does the module handle a question the retrieved context doesn't actually answer?
How does the agent avoid making things up when the context is thin?
How are the sources of an answer collected and shown to the user?
What's the difference between a source and a citation in this module?
How is retrieval quality checked or compared across different settings?
How does the module stay retrieval-only, with the language model living in the app layer?

name: agent-mcp-architecture
goal: Understand the agent and MCP architecture: the single system-prompt assembly point, the task-state machine and memory layers, invariant enforcement, and MCP tool orchestration. Treat buildSystemPrompt as the one assembly point and the task file as the task memory; hold those terms fixed across the dialogue.
questions: How does the agent build the prompt it sends to the model on each turn?
Why are the hard constraints (invariants) placed first in the prompt?
How does the agent remember a task's goal and decisions across many turns?
How does a task move through its stages from planning to done?
How does the agent know when a stage is genuinely finished?
What kinds of memory does the agent keep, and what is each one for?
What happens when a request would break one of the fixed constraints?
How does the conversation history reach the model so it sees the whole dialogue?
How does the agent use external tools through MCP during a turn?
When more than one tool server is connected, how does each call reach the right one?
How does the user profile change the way the agent answers?
How does the agent stay robust when the model returns a malformed response?
How do the pieces — memory, task state, tools, and prompt assembly — fit together?
